GST Registration in Coimbatore

New GST registration, amendments, and composition scheme registration in Coimbatore, filed and tracked end-to-end by an Enrolled GST Practitioner.

Every business crossing the GST threshold — or wanting to claim input tax credit, sell on e-commerce marketplaces, or supply outside Tamil Nadu — needs a GST Identification Number (GSTIN). We handle new registrations, additional place of business amendments, and composition scheme registration for traders, manufacturers, professionals, and service providers across Coimbatore, from document collection to ARN tracking to certificate issuance.

Who needs GST registration, and when

GST registration in India is governed by Sections 22 and 24 of the CGST Act, 2017. Section 22 sets out the turnover-based threshold: registration becomes mandatory once your aggregate turnover in a financial year exceeds ₹40 lakh for suppliers of goods, or ₹20 lakh for suppliers of services (Tamil Nadu is a normal-category state, so the special-category thresholds of ₹20 lakh and ₹10 lakh don’t apply here). Aggregate turnover is computed on an all-India, PAN-wide basis — it includes taxable, exempt, and export supplies, but excludes the GST itself and any inward supplies taxed under reverse charge.

Section 24 overrides the threshold entirely for certain categories, who must register regardless of turnover: businesses making inter-state taxable supplies, casual taxable persons, persons liable to pay tax under reverse charge, e-commerce operators, sellers supplying goods or services through an e-commerce platform (with a narrow exception for notified handicraft and handmade goods sellers), input service distributors, and non-resident taxable persons, among others. A Coimbatore trader who sells only within Tamil Nadu and stays under ₹40 lakh may genuinely not need to register — but the moment that trader lists a product on an online marketplace, registration becomes compulsory from day one, irrespective of turnover.

Once liability arises, Section 22 read with Rule 10 of the CGST Rules requires the application to be filed within 30 days of becoming liable. Many businesses register voluntarily well before crossing the threshold, usually because B2B customers or marketplaces insist on a valid GSTIN before they’ll transact — that’s a legitimate commercial reason, but it’s worth confirming which threshold genuinely applies to you rather than registering reflexively.

Regular registration vs. the composition scheme

Regular registration lets you charge GST on outward supplies, claim input tax credit on eligible purchases, and trade inter-state or online without restriction — at the cost of monthly or quarterly detailed return filing (GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B) plus an annual return. The composition scheme under Section 10 of the CGST Act is available to traders and manufacturers with aggregate turnover up to ₹1.5 crore (₹75 lakh in a few special-category states, not applicable here), and to eligible service providers with turnover up to ₹50 lakh under the separate composition notification for services. Composition dealers pay tax at a flat, lower rate on turnover — typically 1% for traders, 2% for manufacturers, and 6% for the services composition scheme — file a simplified quarterly statement (CMP-08) and one annual return (GSTR-4), but cannot claim input tax credit, cannot supply inter-state, and cannot supply through e-commerce operators required to collect TCS.

The right choice depends on your customer base as much as your turnover. A retail jeweller or textile trader selling almost entirely to local, price-sensitive B2C customers often does well under composition — the lower headline rate and lighter compliance outweighs the lost ITC. A manufacturer or trader whose customers are themselves GST-registered businesses usually needs regular registration, because those customers need a proper tax invoice with ITC to claim their own credit; a composition dealer can’t issue one.

What’s included in our registration service

  • New GST registration for proprietorships, partnerships, LLPs, and companies
  • Composition scheme registration for small traders, manufacturers and eligible service providers
  • Additional place of business and principal place of business amendments
  • Casual and non-resident taxable person registration
  • HSN/SAC code classification for correct tax rate application
  • Post-registration compliance calendar setup so you never miss a due date

Our step-by-step process

  1. Eligibility and structure check — We review your turnover, business activity, and supply pattern to confirm whether regular or composition registration suits you, and whether voluntary registration makes sense even below the threshold.
  2. Document collection and verification — PAN, Aadhaar, business address proof, bank details, and photographs are collected and checked against GSTN's format requirements before filing, which avoids the most common cause of rejection.
  3. ARN generation and application filing — We file Form GST REG-01 on the GST portal, generating your Application Reference Number, and track the application status daily.
  4. Query response — If the jurisdictional officer raises a clarification under Rule 9(2) (Form GST REG-03), we draft and file the documented reply in Form GST REG-04 within the statutory window, which is typically seven working days from the notice.
  5. GSTIN issuance — Once approved, you receive your GSTIN and registration certificate (Form GST REG-06), usually within 7 working days of a clean application or 3 working days where Aadhaar authentication is completed and no physical verification is triggered.
  6. First-return briefing — We walk you through your filing frequency (monthly, or QRMP if eligible), invoice numbering series, and opening stock declarations so your first return cycle starts correctly.

Documents you’ll need

  • PAN card and Aadhaar of proprietor/partners/directors
  • Photograph of applicant(s)
  • Business address proof (electricity bill, rent agreement, or property tax receipt)
  • NOC from property owner, if rented or shared premises
  • Bank account statement or cancelled cheque
  • Partnership deed / MOA & AOA / LLP agreement, as applicable
  • Digital Signature Certificate, for companies and LLPs

Registration nuances by business structure

The documentation and process differ meaningfully depending on how your business is constituted, and this is where generic checklists tend to fall short. A sole proprietorship registers in the individual’s own PAN, which keeps the process simplest but also means there’s no legal separation between business and personal liability — worth knowing before you scale. A partnership firm needs the partnership deed uploaded at registration, and every partner’s PAN, Aadhaar, and photograph; if the firm isn’t registered with the Registrar of Firms, a notarised deed is usually still acceptable but can occasionally draw a clarification query. LLPs and private limited companies need a Digital Signature Certificate for at least one authorised signatory, and the registration application must be signed using that DSC — a step that trips up many first-time founders who assume Aadhaar OTP verification alone is sufficient, the way it is for proprietorships.

For a private limited company or LLP, we also cross-check that the registered office address used for GST matches (or is properly declared as different from) the address on file with the Ministry of Corporate Affairs, since a mismatch here is a common flag during scrutiny years later, not just at registration. Multi-director companies occasionally also need to decide who the “primary authorised signatory” is for day-to-day GST portal access — a decision worth making deliberately rather than defaulting to whoever happens to complete the paperwork first.

What changes immediately after registration

Getting the GSTIN is the beginning of a new set of obligations, not the end of the process. From the date of registration, every tax invoice you issue must carry a specific set of mandatory fields — your GSTIN, a consecutive invoice number, HSN/SAC codes, and the correct tax break-up between CGST/SGST (for intra-state supply) or IGST (for inter-state supply). Your GSTIN and registration certificate must be displayed at your principal place of business, and at every additional place of business you’ve declared. If your turnover crosses the e-invoicing threshold notified for your category of business, invoices need to be reported to the Invoice Registration Portal (IRP) and carry an Invoice Reference Number (IRN) and QR code — a requirement that has progressively extended to smaller turnover bands over successive notifications, so it’s worth checking your applicability rather than assuming it doesn’t apply because it didn’t last year.

You’ll also need to decide your return filing frequency. Businesses with turnover up to ₹5 crore in the preceding financial year can generally opt into the QRMP (Quarterly Return, Monthly Payment) scheme — quarterly GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B, with monthly tax payment via a simple challan in between. Above that threshold, monthly filing is mandatory. We set this up as part of onboarding so your very first return cycle starts on the right frequency, rather than needing correction later.

Should you register voluntarily, before you're required to?

Voluntary registration under the proviso to Section 25(3) is available to anyone below the threshold. The case for it: it lets you claim input tax credit on business purchases from day one, it’s often demanded by B2B customers and marketplaces before they’ll onboard you, and it avoids a scramble later if your turnover grows faster than expected. The case against it: you take on monthly or quarterly return filing and the associated compliance cost even while your turnover is still small, and once registered, cancellation isn’t instant — you can’t simply stop filing if the business slows down. We usually recommend voluntary registration for businesses that expect to cross the threshold within the next financial year, or whose customers specifically require a GSTIN to transact, and recommend waiting for businesses genuinely uncertain about growth trajectory, purely to avoid taking on filing obligations before they’re needed.

Amending your registration after approval

Registration details change — you move premises, add a branch, change your authorised signatory, or add a new business line under the same GSTIN. GST amendments are split into two categories under Rule 19 of the CGST Rules. Non-core field amendments — email ID, mobile number, and a handful of other details — are self-approved instantly on the portal with no officer review. Core field amendments — legal name of the business, principal place of business, and addition or deletion of partners/directors — require filing Form GST REG-14 and are subject to approval by the jurisdictional officer, who can approve, reject, or seek clarification within 15 working days. Adding an additional place of business (a new branch, warehouse, or factory location) is treated as a core amendment and needs supporting address proof for the new location, same as at original registration. We handle amendment filings as they come up rather than letting registration details drift out of date, since a stale address on your GST certificate is a routine but avoidable point of friction during any later scrutiny or refund claim.

Common mistakes that delay or derail an application

In our experience handling registrations across Coimbatore, the same handful of issues account for most delays. An address proof that doesn’t exactly match the applicant’s name — a common issue when a shop runs in a family member’s name but the electricity bill is in another’s — routinely triggers a REG-03 query. A rented premises without a proper NOC from the owner is another frequent trigger, especially for small units taking up space in a shared commercial complex. Photographs that don’t meet the portal’s technical specifications (wrong background, wrong file size) get silently rejected rather than flagged clearly. And HSN/SAC misclassification at the registration stage — picking a generic code instead of the one that actually matches your principal supply — can create rate and ITC-eligibility problems that surface much later, during a scrutiny notice rather than at registration. We check every document against these known failure points before filing, specifically to avoid a back-and-forth with the department.

GST registration across Coimbatore’s industries and areas

Coimbatore’s registration profile varies meaningfully by industry, and that shapes how we approach each application. Spinning mills and textile processing units along the Tirupur Road and Avinashi Road belt are almost always regular-registration cases from day one — their customers are other registered manufacturers and exporters who need full ITC, and many units also supply inter-state, which removes any threshold question entirely. Jewellers around RS Puram and Big Bazaar Street sit in an unusual position: high per-transaction value but often largely cash-and-carry retail to individual customers, which means the registration decision (and later, the ITC and reverse-charge treatment on old gold purchased from unregistered individuals) needs careful handling rather than a template approach.

IT and services businesses in Saravanampatti and Peelamedu frequently cross the lower ₹20 lakh services threshold quickly even as small teams, and many also export services to clients outside India — which brings zero-rated supply and LUT (Letter of Undertaking) considerations into the registration conversation from the outset. Traders around Gandhipuram and Town Hall — Coimbatore’s traditional wholesale and trading hub — more often weigh the composition-versus-regular decision seriously, since their customer mix of both registered dealers and cash retail buyers cuts both ways. Engineering and auto-component manufacturers in Singanallur and Ganapathy typically need multiple GST registrations if they operate more than one factory or warehouse location within the state, since each additional place of business needs to be declared, and sometimes separately registered depending on how operations are structured.

If registration is rejected and you disagree with the reason

A rejection under Form GST REG-05, after a REG-03 query has been answered unsatisfactorily in the officer’s view, isn’t always the end of the road, and it isn’t always correctly reasoned. In most cases the practical route is simply to file a fresh application correcting the actual deficiency — there’s no limit on reapplications, and it’s usually faster than litigating a rejection. But where the rejection itself appears to misapply the law — for instance, rejecting a residential-address application in a locality where residential-as-principal-place-of-business is genuinely permitted — the applicant has the right to appeal the rejection order before the Appellate Authority under Section 107 of the CGST Act, within three months of the order. We assess each rejection on its specific reasoning before recommending which route makes more sense for your situation.

GST registration and Udyam (MSME) registration

These are separate registrations serving different purposes, and Coimbatore’s manufacturing and services MSMEs often need both. GST registration is a tax compliance requirement tied to turnover and supply type; Udyam registration is a classification (micro, small, or medium) that unlocks priority-sector lending, delayed-payment protection under the MSME Development Act, and eligibility for various state and central subsidy schemes. Since Udyam registration itself is linked to your GSTIN and PAN for turnover verification, getting GST registration right — and keeping your GST return filings current — directly affects how smoothly Udyam registration and its periodic re-validation go. We typically sequence GST registration first, then handle Udyam registration as a closely connected next step for eligible manufacturing and services clients.

Frequently asked questions about GST Registration

How long does GST registration take in Coimbatore?

With complete documents, Aadhaar authentication, and no department query, registration is typically granted within 3-7 working days. If a clarification notice (REG-03) is issued, factor in another 7-15 days for the reply and re-review.

Is GST registration mandatory for my business?

It's mandatory once your aggregate turnover crosses ₹40 lakh for goods or ₹20 lakh for services (Tamil Nadu thresholds under Section 22), or if you fall under one of the compulsory categories in Section 24 — inter-state supply, e-commerce selling, reverse charge liability, and others — regardless of turnover.

What's the difference between regular and composition GST registration?

Composition dealers pay tax at a flat, lower rate on turnover and file a simplified quarterly statement plus one annual return, but cannot claim input tax credit, cannot supply inter-state, and cannot sell through e-commerce operators. Regular registration allows full ITC claims and unrestricted trade but requires monthly or quarterly detailed return filing.

Can I register GST without a commercial address in Coimbatore?

Yes — a residential address with proper ownership or rental proof and a utility bill is acceptable as a principal place of business, subject to local zoning norms for certain trades.

What happens if my GST application gets rejected?

We analyse the rejection reason, correct the deficiency — usually an address-proof, NOC, or classification issue — and refile. A fresh ARN is generated for the resubmission, and there's no cap on how many times you can reapply.

Do I need GST registration for an online business run from home?

Yes, if you sell through e-commerce operators like Amazon or Flipkart, GST registration is mandatory irrespective of turnover under Section 24, since marketplace facilitators require a valid GSTIN before onboarding sellers (a narrow exemption exists for notified handicraft/handmade goods sellers below the threshold).

Should I choose composition or regular registration for a new shop in Coimbatore?

It depends mainly on your customers, not just your turnover. If most of your sales are to registered businesses that need ITC on their purchase, regular registration usually works better despite the extra compliance. If you sell mainly to individual, price-sensitive customers and stay within the ₹1.5 crore composition limit, the lower flat rate and simpler filing often wins.

How much does GST registration cost?

Government filing itself is free; our professional fee covers document vetting, application filing, query handling, and the first-return briefing so you start compliant from day one.
